Sentence: You step down the narrow terrace, boots finding the patched concrete by habit.
Salt and diesel cling to the air beneath the morning heat, an acrid tang that presses behind your teeth.
Your hand drifts to
the salvaged brass compass at your chest—not because it points any truer now, but because its weight steadies the quick ache behind your ribs.
sand mixed with scoured shell in the creases where doors meet thresholds.
Someone has shoved a flattened tarp under a door and tied a
cord.
Across the row, a bird's wing catches a laundry line and the cloth snaps like a small flag.
You run a mental checklist as you walk.
Sandbags—repaired.
Diversion channels—cleared.
Rooftop rain barrels—topped.
The motions are automatic because they have to be.
Your auburn braid, threaded with shell and seed beads, brushes the collar of
your worn olive jacket; the elbow patch on the left sleeve is a pale crescent of mended hope.
You taste salt.
You blink it away.
You were an engineer once who wore crisp lab coats and presented models in blue-lit auditoriums.
You came home when the grants ran thin and your family’s roofs started to sag.
You came back because the

plans the city signed off on two administrations ago never matched the shoreline you remembered—because your childhood friends raise seedlings on tarps and your brother sprays stencils of tiny hands across concrete.
You keep telling yourself
the work is the same; only the audience is different.
Rafi is already at the nursery rooftop when you climb the ladder.
The greenhouse domes glow with held heat; the scent of wet soil and cut basil folds into the diesel.
He’s crouched over a tray of saplings, gloved hands patient and deliberate.
Morning's heavy.
Tide came in farther than last week's, but the barrels held.
Lio patched one of the eastern levee pockets.
He's got your paint all over his hoodie again.
You kneel beside him, palms caked with compost.
Your fingers like the feel of the soil remind you of why this place still matters.
Did you get electricity from the panels for the pump last night?
Rafi (shrugs) Barely.
Solar's decent when it's calm, but the clouds came in.
I ran the hand pump at midnight.
Mrs.
Ortega says she'd rather help than leave.
You twist the rim of a seedling pot between your fingers, slow.
The rooftop hums with small life—bees, a rooftop cat that watches with baleful interest, the soft ticking of a makeshift water gauge.
• Run my thumb along the compass, pretending it still points
You trace the compass rim.
It doesn't move, but the ritual calms the tightness at your throat.
• Let the braid fall free, tuck it behind your ear and focus on the seedlings
You tuck the braid back.
Dirt clings to your fingertips; the saplings look sharper somehow, immediate and salvageable.
The narrative continues as the rooftop conversation carries on.
Rafi glances up at you, the lines at the corner of his eyes deepening.
Mayor's feed said 'expedited process' again.
Corporate ads all over the plaza screens.
Glossy renders.
It's like they're selling a resort instead of a wall.
You feel that thin low-burning—impatience masked as a steady ache.
You hate the word 'expedited' when it applies to other people's homes.
Expedited for them means less time for us to respond.
Less time for alternatives.
It means bulldozers before conversations, probably.
We can still call the neighbors.
Get a meeting going.
Dr.
Sima said she'd look at the models again.
She thinks—' (he hesitates) '—there are maybe modifications we can argue for.
Maybe,
small hands reaching for a tide line.
Paint flecked across the concrete.
The sight gives you a tight, half-smile.
Lio appears at the ladder mouth, dripping a thin rope of spray paint from his fingers like a badge.
You see?
I moved the tide line up a bit.
Make it scarier.
You laugh, a dry sound.
Don't give the kids nightmares.
They're already living it.
Besides, the news needs emotion—graphs don't make people cry.
You meet his eyes—sun-faded, earnest, proud.
He leans his shoulder against the railing and watches the city waking up below the promenade.
You gonna tell them to calm down about the wall here?
Or will you make a big speech like Sima?
Neither.
I'll tell them the facts.
Then I'll tell them what those facts mean for us.
He snorts.
Promises, Maya.
Promises with an engineer's footnote.
You feel the humor but also the weight.
You want to be blunt and right, not showy.
The rooftop smells of basil and salt and a city adjusting to a new normal.
Mrs.
Ortega with flour-dusted hands, a pair of fishermen with sun-creased faces, teenagers with paint on their sleeves, a young family clutching a stroller.
The room settles with the patient noise of people who have made too many emergency plans.
Thanks for coming.
I know mornings are busy.
I know 'expedited' isn't a word any of us wanted to hear.
We'll keep this short and useful.
A murmur of agreement.
Someone coughs.
The fan clicks.
You open the first map.
It shows the proposed seawall in glossy charcoal, a line that eats the wetlands in a clean, economical arc.
reduce the spinning worry into plottable items.
The seawall as proposed cuts through the marsh corridor here.' (You tap the map.
Fingers steady.) 'Where the city needs green space most.
It would protect the transit hub, yes, but it would require dismantling these homes and filling the wetlands.
Mrs.
Ortega But they'll tell us it's for the greater good.
More safety for the whole city.
Maybe.
But 'greater good' often gets measured in dollar-per-meter.
The models Dr.
Sima ran show us the hydrology changing—faster wave reflection, higher erosion downcoast.
It protects a section, and it damages another.
Murmurs rise and fall like a small tide.
We can present a hybrid plan.
Small reinforced nodes here, coupled with marsh restoration.
It spreads the load.
Fisherman Who'll pay for that?
Maya Corvin (this time you let your voice show the impatience you've held thin all morning) That's the hard part.
We need to show it works—show the metrics.
Make the cost arguments.
Make the human ones.
A teenager Won't the city listen if it's cheaper?
Sometimes.
Sometimes not.
Sometimes the city listens only when the public pressure is sharp enough.
numbers, deadlines, cost-benefit language.
That's the engineering brain in you—old habits of model and projection.
• Start with technical diagrams, let the numbers do the convincing
You lay out the graphs and projections.
Eyes track the lines; a couple of heads lean forward.
Technical clarity settles like a balm over the room.
• Begin with a personal story—Mrs.
Ortega's house, Lio's stencils—to make it human first
You begin with Mrs.
Ortega's memory of the old tide clock.
Faces soften; someone reaches for your hand.
The technical details still wait, but the room's attention is slower, warmer.
The meeting proceeds with both technical and personal points being addressed.
The door opens and Dr.
Sima steps in, carrying a tablet and the tired look of someone who has been in too many late-night model runs.
She sets her glasses back into place with the tape on the bridge and offers a small, tired smile.
Morning.
I ran the models overnight with the new boundary conditions.
Some of the worst-case projections are—' (she exhales, searching for words) '—not good.
But there are multiple feasible scenarios that could reduce displacement if the construction is rerouted.
blue gradients that mark likely inundation, green streaks of surviving marsh, red patches where homes would go.
Translate, Sima.
if the wall goes in as designed, expect more concentrated wave energy to the south.
That means erosion and the likely loss of the eastern terrace over decades.
If we include corridors—spaces that allow water to funnel and wetlands to breathe—the system dissipates energy better.
Immediate construction costs rise, but long-term maintenance drops.
Neighbor So we lose some homes now or we lose more later.
There's no free pass.' (she meets your eyes) 'Engineering is trade-offs.
I can give you the numbers.
I can show confidence intervals.
I can't tell you which homes are worth less to history.
You translate again, slower, because people need both the numbers and the meaning.
We don't have an answer yet.
We have options.
We have trade-offs.
We have a clock that's been moved forward by the Mayor's bulletin.
The municipal timeline is going to force decisions quicker than we like.
You fold the maps carefully.
The room is quiet in that particular, raw way—people holding futures in their palms.
A neighbor raises a hand, voice small Who comes to the hearings?
Do we need to speak?
Will that matter?
Yes.
We need to speak.
Not just at one hearing—at every public forum.
We make our case in language they understand and in numbers they respect.
We show the neighborhoods that will be impacted.
We make alliances with people outside Low Row who see the long-term costs.
Lio's fingers drum on his knee.
What about the corporate renderings?
They're slick.
People get dazzled.
We'll counter the dazzle with the lived-in detail.
We'll put up our own visuals—photos, testimonies.
We'll show how the marsh works, not just how a wall looks.
People respond to what they can imagine living in.
The ticking fan sounds louder for a beat.
You press your palm to the table to steady yourself.
You are impatient—always impatient—because the work needs to move faster than the machinery of politics allows.
But the
intensity is low; there's a slow, careful sorrow under everything, like a tide pool holding a reflected sky.
I'll prepare the model runs in public-friendly formats.
Give me the specs you want emphasized.
And—' (she hesitates) '—I'll testify about the uncertainty.
Not to frighten, but to be honest.
Good.
We'll make a timeline for outreach.
Rafi, you coordinate volunteers.
Lio, keep the street art coming, but make it clear where the facts are posted.
Mrs.
Ortega, can you organize the food for evening meetings?
Mrs.
Ortega Of course.
You say when.
You ball your hands into fists inside your jacket and let them unclench slowly.
The room organizes itself like a small organism acknowledging a threat.
EXPEDITED HEARINGS SCHEDULED.
CORPORATE DELEGATION ARRIVING.
It moves with bureaucratic indifference while a boy across the street chases a stray dog through ankle-high water.
You feel the pressure increase, subtle as a rising tide—still a slow swell, but inexorable.
They'll send their team.
They'll have renderings and lawyers and a media plan.
They always do.
Then we prepare on our terms.
We shape the narrative.
We bring science and stories together.
We make sure the hearings are more than a checkbox.
Dr.
Sima (soft) Also—be careful with promises.
Politicians and companies sometimes weaponize good intentions.
Keep the community's agency central.
Lio's future, Mrs.
Ortega's kitchen, children who have only ever known these terraces.
Your sea-glass eyes
sting—not from wind, but from the chronic low ache of knowing how little time neighborhoods often get.
who will speak where, who will take notes, who will photograph the marsh at low tide.
The collective low hum of organized worry fills the room.
You catch Lio's eye on your way out.
He gives you a brief, wry nod—half challenge, half plea.
Don't let them make this a done deal.
Not before we speak.
I won't let them.
You step outside into the heat and salty air, the city opening before you like a problem with too many possible fixes and not enough hands.
The diesel and the distant machinery hum again.
The municipal
bulletin on the plaza screen flickers.
For a long second you simply stand and breathe, counting the small, deliberate steps you will have to take.
You feel a low tightening, like the moment before rain when the air holds its breath.
It's not panic.
It's not frantic energy.
It's the steady, grim recognition that the next moves are limited and consequential—and that you are already committed to making them.
You glance at your compass one last time, fingers curling around the tarnished brass.
The needle does not move.
You tuck it back beneath your jacket, braid brushing your palm.
You step into the municipal hall still carrying the heat of the Low Row on your skin, the salt clinging to the cuffs of your jacket.
The place is cool in a way that feels intentional
— air conditioning, cleaned edges, a kind of institutional detachment that smooths the city’s rougher sounds into manageable data points.
not panic, but a slow, corrosive worry that eats at the edges of confidence.
You imagine the marsh in diagram lines, the community garden roofs, Lio’s paint-splattered hoodie on a future that might be half-erased.
Good morning.
Thank you for coming early.
We want this to be efficient.
Efficient at what cost?
At protecting the city.
At moving funds that keep people dry.
We are trying to balance immediacy and feasibility.
You study her scarf, the way she tucks it against her neck, as if warmth and optics can be the same thing.
The atrium smells faintly of coffee and lemon polish.
It feels like standing under a glass dome when outside the city is being rewritten in saltwater.
• Step closer and speak plainly
Maya Corvin closes the distance, steadier than she feels.
'Feasibility for whom, Mayor?
The wall's timeline makes zero room for the community design we’ve built.' Her words land and the Mayor’s smile tightens for a fraction longer.
• Keep your distance and listen
Maya Corvin stays where she is, arms folded over her prints, letting the Mayor and her aides outline the agenda.
Listening keeps her in control; she measures the gaps she’ll have to fill later.
Continue with Elias Kahn arriving.
Maya.
He gives you a look that says he knows your stakes.
I read your summaries.
I know what you and Rafi have been doing.
Then you know we need more than the narrow window they’re offering.
Elias shifts, glances at a staffer, then lowers his voice.

The legal team says a turnkey seawall is the most defensible route.
Defensible for them.
What about the livelihoods we’re trying to keep?
The wetland corridors?
The nursery roofs?
I sympathize.
I do.
But consensus matters in these votes.
If we push too hard publicly without a plan that looks 'municipal-ready,' Mayor Velez will go with the path that produces the fewest legal headaches.
his carefulness against your impatience.

You already have technical rigor.
Dr.
Sima agrees with your models.

I can help translate it.

You want to ask whether promises are enough.
You want to press until something gives.
Instead you press a hand to your map, feeling the paper between your fingers like battlements.
immaculate jacket, blade-sharp bob, steel-gray eyes that appraise you as though you were an object set before a problem to solve.

Your footprints clean the map of messy neighborhoods and living systems until nothing recognizable is left.

(he searches for language that will hold)

funding tranches, indemnities, investor confidence, legal defensibility.

• Call out Kai directly on the human cost

For a second you see something like recognition and then she answers, 'We calculate loss and lives saved.
Sentiment doesn’t alter raw mortality in a storm.' The answer lands like flint.

proof, models, certainty.

translate the community plan into budget stages, prepare technical appendices, marshal voices to fill a hearing room, and coax municipal law into tolerating hybridity.

the city’s machinery is efficient at

It is very good at making decisions that look clean on paper and mean ruin for people whose lives cannot be folded into spreadsheets.
You make a choice in the little, private place where resolve hardens into action.
We will demand time.
We will make them see that community plans are not sentimental — they are survival strategies.
We will fill the room.
If they set the clock, we will set the agenda.
It is not a triumphant decision.
It feels heavy and necessary — a small, stubborn defiance edged with the knowledge that saying 'no' to the machine will cost energy, sleep, and perhaps relationships.

mobilizing your neighbors feels like lighting a line of candles in a hall full of floodlights.
You head back toward the Low Row, the city’s smell changing as you descend — diesel and seaweed and the exact, stubborn scent of life kept going despite everything.
The day is not brighter.
It is only truer.

Rafi with his clay-stained gloves, Lio with his paint-splattered hoodie, Mrs.

be enough.
not bright, but dense and useful.

braid is tucked under your collar; the compass is quiet in your jacket.

tent.

The wall reduces immediate flood risk along key nodes.

If we remove or truncate those marsh corridors, we see long-term amplification of risk.

the air thickens, people lean forward.

it lets faces form in the listeners' minds.)

cross-sections of a seawall integrated with tidal marsh inserts, diagrams of stepped retreats, and cost curves that show phased investment.

a mother's hand tightens around her child's shoulder when she sees the photo; an analyst in the back leans forward at the charts.

measured, practiced, a man comfortable smoothing edges.

stop the bleeding now, worry about the scarring later.

not a yes, not a no, but a forced timeline that compresses options.

phone trees, petitions, design revisions, fundraising, bargaining.
